zotero.abstractNote: Brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) have the potential to revolutionize human-computer interaction. However, their widespread adoption is hindered by the need for extensive calibration and the reliance on expensive, specialized hardware. In this paper, we present MYND, a framework for the unsupervised evaluation of novel BCI control strategies on consumer-grade hardware. We demonstrate that our approach allows for the rapid development and testing of BCI applications without the need for time-consuming calibration sessions. Our results show that MYND achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art supervised methods while significantly reducing the burden on the user.
